findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've nailed the market opportunity, but investors will want to hear directly from the people who actually need this—add a slide showing 3-4 customer conversations or pilot results that prove developers and villa owners are frustrated enough to switch. For example: "Interviewed 12 villa developers in Dubai; 10 cited reliability as top blocker to solar adoption" or "Pilot with [Developer Name]: their legacy system failed twice in 18 months, costing $15K in repairs—they committed to testing SolarisKit." This transforms your mandate story from "the market should want this" to "the market is actively looking for this."

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the *what* (mandates, regulations, pipeline) but you're missing the *when* — add a timeline showing when these regulations actually take effect and when the villa pipeline hits peak construction, so investors see the urgency isn't theoretical. For example, if Estidama enforcement tightens in 2025 and the developer pipeline peaks in 2026-2027, lead with "Regulatory enforcement window: 2025-2027" and tie it directly to why waiting another year costs you market share.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature list—you need to show *how* the AI actually works on a real problem. Add a concrete example: show the exact inputs (e.g., "villa in Dubai, 4 occupants, flat roof, hard water") and walk through what HelioHelp outputs (e.g., "recommends 12-collector system, predicts 18,000 kWh/year savings, flags hard-water polymer requirement, generates installer checklist for high-wind installation"). This transforms it from "AI-guided installation" into proof that your system can actually solve the deployment complexity that's slowing your growth today.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You're building a platform that depends on AI to scale globally, but you haven't mentioned what cloud infrastructure, APIs, or third-party services power HelioHelp — this matters to investors because it affects your unit economics, scalability ceiling, and risk profile. Add a single sentence to Slide 19 stating something like "HelioHelp runs on [AWS/Azure/GCP] with [specific services: e.g., computer vision APIs for site assessment, weather data from OpenWeather, geospatial analysis via Mapbox]" so investors understand your technical stack and can assess whether you're dependent on any single provider or service that could constrain growth.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi, EU) with mandated solar installations and storing customer data through HelioHelp — add a slide in the Appendix that covers your data handling approach, certifications (ISO 27001 or equivalent), and compliance with local regulations like ADISA or GDPR where relevant, so investors know you've thought through the operational and legal risks before they ask.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue, gross profit, and EBITDA figures, and mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and 'GTM via developers/ESCOs ensures scalable revenue from 2026,' but do not explicitly state detailed assumptions such as growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-slide assumptions breakdown that shows: (1) how you're reaching 2030 revenue—for example, "Base case assumes 50 units in 2026, growing 3x annually to 1,200 units by 2030 via developer partnerships," (2) your hardware cost structure—"35-45% gross margin assumes $X COGS per unit at scale, with UAE manufacturing reducing costs by 20% vs. imported alternatives," and (3) how licensing kicks in—"Licensing revenue begins 2028 assuming 2-3 OEM partnerships at $X per unit." This transforms your current "conservative projections" claim into credible, testable math

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see the full unit economics story—specifically CAC and LTV for your core customer segments (hotels vs. villas vs. utilities). Add a simple two-row table on Slide 14 showing: Hotels—CAC $X, LTV $Y, payback Z months; Villas—CAC $X, LTV $Y, payback Z months. This lets investors immediately see which segment is most capital-efficient and why your model scales.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy between the $213K contracted amount and the $300K projected revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted amount on Slide 12 is your actual near-term revenue, but your 2026 projection on Slide 14 shows $300K—clarify whether that $300K includes pipeline deals beyond the two signed customers, or if it's an error. Add a footnote on Slide 14 stating something like "2026 hardware revenue of $300K assumes Rixos ($207K) + EtihadWE ($6K) contracted deals plus $87K from high-value discussions expected to close by Q2 2026" so investors see the bridge between what's locked and what's projected.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy this new funding round—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing scale-up: $X to expand Ras Al Khaimah facility," "Sales & BD team: $X for 3 hires," "Working capital for Rixos + EtihadWE deployments: $X," "R&D for HelioFlow v2: $X") with months of runway each bucket provides. Right now investors see your $350K seed gave you 18 months to 50 installations, but they have no idea what you're asking for in this round or how it accelerates that timeline.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line under "Rixos (live project)" stating the contract value or monthly revenue being generated—something like "Rixos: 500 units installed, $120K contracted revenue" or "EtihadWE: Live since Q3 2024, $45K MRR"—so investors see actual money flowing in, not just project names.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual revenue momentum—add specific numbers like "Q3 revenue: $180K, Q4: $240K (+33% MoM)" or "Rixos project generated $85K in first three months, tracking to $120K by month six" so they can see this isn't just potential, it's already moving.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a killer proof point with Rixos staying operational through the December storm while competitors failed—now add one sentence to that timeline showing the customer renewed or expanded their commitment because of that reliability, or mention the O&M contract value you're capturing from them. This transforms a one-off win into evidence that your product keeps customers locked in long-term, which is exactly what investors need to see for unit economics to hold up.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Replace "Legacy Solar," "Heat Pumps," and "Electric Storage" with actual competitor names — for example, "Sunrun Solar," "Carrier Heat Pump," and "Tesla Powerwall" — so investors immediately understand who you're beating and why your specific positioning matters. Right now the table reads as theoretical; naming real players makes your advantage concrete and credible.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, with competitors shown as weaker (▲ or ●). No explanation is provided for why SolarisKit achieves superior performance across all these disparate technical dimensions.

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why you're better than existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a regional HVAC giant decides to build a flat-roof heat pump—they have distribution, capital, and brand trust you don't. Add a slide that names 1-2 specific incumbents, explains why their business model makes it hard for them to move fast into this niche (for example: "Carrier's margin structure depends on $15K+ installations; our $8K price point breaks their dealer economics"), and then state your defensibility window in months or years—something like "We have 18 months to own the GCC market before a

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors believe you can execute at scale—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and validate your strategy. For example, add a line like "Strategic Advisor: [Name], former VP Manufacturing at [relevant solar/cleantech company]" or "Board Observer: [Name], led go-to-market for [comparable exit]"—people who've done exactly what you're about to do and can vouch for your approach.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" next to his bio. Investors need to know you're committed long-term and that equity is structured to protect the company, so even a brief mention signals you've thought this through and aren't planning an early exit.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification and patents locked down, but investors in regulated markets will want to see explicit alignment with the three mandates you mention—Estidama, Dubai, and Barjeel—spelled out on your compliance slide (Appendix B). Add a single row showing which specific requirement each regulation addresses (e.g., "Estidama: Thermal efficiency standards — SolarisKit exceeds X% efficiency"; "Dubai: Flat-roof compatibility mandate — purpose-built design"; "Barjeel: Hard-water durability — polymer absorber rated for Y years"), so it's crystal clear your product isn't just compliant but purpose-built for these exact rules.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down across three jurisdictions and a solid risk mitigation section, but investors will want to know explicitly: are there any pending patent challenges, trademark disputes, or licensing conflicts that could affect your IP moat or the Rixos deployment? Add a single bullet to your IP defense slide (Slide 8) stating something like "No pending litigation or IP disputes; all patents and trademarks registered without opposition" — this removes doubt and signals clean legal standing, especially important since you're claiming HelioFlow as a core defensibility asset.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these match. However, Slide 6 states '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' against a '$400M Segment' for UAE Villas & Developers, which would imply 5% of $400M = $20M, but this is internally consistent. The inconsistency is that Slide 1 references a single $207K contract, yet Slide 6 projects $20M annual revenue from 5% market share — a 97x difference that is not explained by timeline or scaling factors in the provided slides.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete slide deck
findings.suggestion: Go through the entire deck systematically and replace every instance of [URL_REDACTED] and [UNKNOWN] with actual content — Slide 3's title should read "Solution" or "Product" (not [UNKNOWN]), and every [URL_REDACTED] placeholder needs either a real source citation, image, or removed entirely if it's not essential. Also fix the cut-off text on Slide 6 where "Strong push for dec" should read "Strong push for decarbonisation" to match the full sentence in your footnotes. Run a final spell-check pass across all slides — these gaps signal incompleteness to investors and distract from your genuinely strong story.
